coredata.dc:title: Generalized ratiometric fluorescence nanosensors based on carbon dots and an advanced chemometric model
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Xiu-Fang
Chen, Zeng-Ping
Huang, Ying
Kang, Chao
Yu, Ru-Qin
coredata.dc:description:
Probe encapsulated by biologically localized embedding (PEBBLE) has emerged as a new type of sensing technique for complex systems. Generalized ratiometric PEBBLE nanosensors prepared by encapsulating an intensity-based probe and an inert reference dye inside the pores of stable matrix possess advantages of easy synthesis, immunity to interference, lower toxicity, and robustness to variations in probe loading. However, the selection of appropriate reference dyes used in generalized ratiometric PEBBLE nanosensors is a rather difficult task since they should satisfy some stringent requirements. In this contribution, the feasibility of using carbon dots (C-dots) as generic inert references in synthesizing PEBBLE nanosensors was first investigated in detail. And a dual-wavelength monitoring strategy and the quantitative fluorescence model for generalized ratiometric probes (QFMGRP) were adopted to solve the problems brought by the use of carbon dots as inert references. C-dots doped PEBBLE nanosensors (C-PEBBLE nanosensors) for the quantification of
NO
2
−
and free Ca2+ were synthesized by encapsulating C-dots and intensity based fluorescence probes (i.e., acriflavine for
NO
2
−
, and Rhod-2 for Ca2+, respectively) inside the pores of stable matrix. Experimental results showed that the combination of C-PEBBLEs, the QFMGRP model and the dual-wavelength monitoring strategy achieved accurate quantification of
NO
2
−
and the free Ca2+ in real-world samples. Their quantitative results were in good consistence with those determined by HPLC and atomic absorption spectrophotometer, respectively. The strategies proposed in this contribution have generic applicability in the synthesis of PEBBLE nanosensors and their quantitative applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Inert reference dye
PEBBLE nanosensors
Carbon dots
Quantitative fluorescence model
Dual-wavelength

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of C-dots doped PEBBLE (C-PEBBLE) nanosensor

NO

in samples using the C-PEBBLE nanosensor composed of the C-dots and acriflavine

Characterization of the C-dots and C-PEBBLE nanosensors

Quantitative determination of
NO
2
−
using the C-PEBBLE nanosensors composed of the C-dots and acriflavine
Quantification of the free Ca2+ using the C-PEBBLE nanosensor encapsulating the C-dots and Rhod-2

a) TEM image of C-dots, b) image of water (left) and C-dots (right) under 365 nm UV lamp, respectively.

NO
2
−
(a) and the free Ca2+ (b), respectively.

NO
2
−
, b) the fluorescence intensities of acriflavine solution (2.093 μM) at 452 nm in the presence of different kinds of interferences.

.

NO
2

in the real-world environmental water sample and spiked samples.

NO

in the real-world environmental water sample and spiked samples obtained by different models in combination with the C-PEBBLE nanosensor containing both the C-dots and acriflavine.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The expected concentrations of the free Ca2+ were calculated from the quantitative results of atomic absorption spectrophotometer (AAS).

head.ce:title: Generalized ratiometric fluorescence nanosensors based on carbon dots and an advanced chemometric model

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Tobacco Quality Research of Guizhou Province, College of Tobacco Science, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Tobacco Quality Research of Guizhou Province, College of Tobacco Science, Guizhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guizhou University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Probe encapsulated by biologically localized embedding (PEBBLE) has emerged as a new type of sensing technique for complex systems. Generalized ratiometric PEBBLE nanosensors prepared by encapsulating an intensity-based probe and an inert reference dye inside the pores of stable matrix possess advantages of easy synthesis, immunity to interference, lower toxicity, and robustness to variations in probe loading. However, the selection of appropriate reference dyes used in generalized ratiometric PEBBLE nanosensors is a rather difficult task since they should satisfy some stringent requirements. In this contribution, the feasibility of using carbon dots (C-dots) as generic inert references in synthesizing PEBBLE nanosensors was first investigated in detail. And a dual-wavelength monitoring strategy and the quantitative fluorescence model for generalized ratiometric probes (QFMGRP) were adopted to solve the problems brought by the use of carbon dots as inert references. C-dots doped PEBBLE nanosensors (C-PEBBLE nanosensors) for the quantification of
NO
2
−
and free Ca2+ were synthesized by encapsulating C-dots and intensity based fluorescence probes (i.e., acriflavine for
NO
2
−
, and Rhod-2 for Ca2+, respectively) inside the pores of stable matrix. Experimental results showed that the combination of C-PEBBLEs, the QFMGRP model and the dual-wavelength monitoring strategy achieved accurate quantification of
NO
2
−
and the free Ca2+ in real-world samples. Their quantitative results were in good consistence with those determined by HPLC and atomic absorption spectrophotometer, respectively. The strategies proposed in this contribution have generic applicability in the synthesis of PEBBLE nanosensors and their quantitative applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Since probe encapsulated by biologically localized embedding (PEBBLE) was firstly proposed by Clark and Kopelman in 1998 [1], it has emerged as a new type of fluorescence sensing and imaging technique for complex systems. PEBBLE nanosensor encapsulating an intensity-based fluorescence indicator and an inert reference fluorescence dye inside the pores of stable matrix (typically made from polyacrylamide and decyl methacrylate) can be used as a generalized ratiometric fluorescent probe. Generalized ratiometric probes based on PEBBLE technique possess not only the advantages of traditional ratiometric fluorescent probes over intensity-based fluorescent probes such as self-calibration, dual-signaling strategy, robustness to variations in probes loading, cell membrane thickness and maldistribution of probe in different cells [2–4], but also some unique advantages such as easy synthesis, immunity to interference, lower toxicity to biological tissues and cells and wider applications [5]. PEBBLE nanosensors have been successfully applied to the analysis of pH, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, oxygen and glucose in cells [6–10].

Though it is relatively easy to encapsulate an intensity-based probe and an inert reference dye inside stable matrix by microemulsion polymerization, the selection of an appropriate inert reference dye is not a trivial task in synthesizing a conventional generalized ratiometric PEBBLE nanosensor. The inert reference dye used in a conventional generalized ratiometric PEBBLE nanosensor should satisfy the following requirements. First of all, the inert reference dye should be insensitive to the analytes of interest. It should also have a relatively large molecular weight or large molecular size to prevent leaching of the dye from the micropores of PEBBLE nanosensor. Moreover, either the excitation or emission spectrum of the inert reference dye should have certain degree of overlapping with the corresponding spectrum of the intensity-based fluorescence probe encapsulated inside PEBBLE nanosensor to ensure that both the inert dye and the intensity-based probe can be excited by light of the same wavelength, or their excitation spectra can be recorded at the same emission wavelength. However, the application of traditional univariate ratiometric model to quantitative analyze the spectral measurements obtained by the using of generalized ratiometric PEBBLE nanosensor requires that either the emission or excitation spectrum of the inert fluorescence dye should be overlapped as little as possible with the corresponding spectrum of the intensity-based probe. These rather stringent as well as somewhat conflicting requirements hinder wider application of PEBBLE nanosensors.

The key to further development of PEBBLE nanosensors lies in the availability of inert reference dyes satisfying the first two requirements and a new calibration strategy which can relieve the conflicting requirements on spectral overlapping. Carbon dots (C-dots) have many attractive properties such as strong anti-photobleaching, excellent water-solubility, adjustable emission spectrum regulated by changing the synthesis conditions, high fluorescence quantum yield, low biological toxicity and good biocompatibility, and have been widely applied to the fields of light-emitting diode, biological sensing and imaging [11–14]. They are good candidates for inert references used in PEBBLE nanosensors. As for the conflicting requirements on spectral overlapping, some of the present authors have recently proposed a quantitative fluorescence model for generalized ratiometric probes based on PEBBLE technique (QFMGRP) [15], which can tolerate moderate degree of overlapping between either the emission or excitation spectra of the inert fluorescence dye and the intensity-based probe used in PEBBLE nanosensors. In this contribution, C-dots were first used as inert references in the synthesis of generalized ratiometric PEBBLE nanosensors. And the QFMGRP model was further developed to accommodate the situation where both the excitation and emission spectra of C-dots (served as the inert reference dye) are completely separated from the corresponding ones of the intensity-based probe encapsulated inside PEBBLE nanosensors (in other words, it is impossible to simultaneously obtain the fluorescence signals of both the C-dots and the intensity-based probe). Therefore, the combination of C-dots and the extended QFMGRP can simplify the synthesis of PEBBLE nanosensors and widen their applications.

Citric acid (99.5%), ethylenediamine (99.5%), dioctyl sulfosuccinate sodium (AOT, 96%), N, N′-methylenebisacrylamide (≥99.0%), polyoxyethylene (4) lauryl ether (Brij 30, average Mn ~362), acrylamide (99.9%), acriflavine (13.3–15.8% chlorine), ammonium persulfate (APS, 99.99%), N,N,N,N-tetramethylethylenediamine (TEMED, 99%), sodium nitrite (99.99%), hydroxyethl piperazine ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES, ≥99.5%), Ethylenebis(oxyethylenenitrilo)tetraacetic acid (EGTA, analytical grade), and calcium chloride anhydrous (99.99%) were all purchased from Aladdin Regent (Shanghai, China) and used without further purification. Rhod-2 (tripotassium salt) was purchased from Life Technologies (Shanghai, China). Child-Life nature orange flavor liquid calcium/magnesium (Los Angeles, USA) was obtained from local supermarket. Ultra-pure water used throughout the experiments was produced by an Aquapro water purification system (Aquapro, China).

Water-soluble C-dots were synthesized according to the protocol reported in literatures [16,17]. The experimental procedures were briefly described as follows. 10.0 g critic acid and 3.84 g ethylenediamine were dissolved in 100 mL ultra-pure water. 6 mL of the resulting solution was transferred into a 25 mL serum bottle with a hole on the lid, and heated for 2 min in a microwave oven with a power of 700 W (Galanz, China) to obtain some brown foam-like stuff, i.e., raw C-dots. The obtained raw C-dots were dispersed in 5 mL ultra-pure water with the aid of ultrasonic vibration, and then transferred to a 500 Da molecular weight cut-of dialysis bag. After 24 h of dialysis against ultra-pure water (water was renewed once every 3–4 h) for desalination and ultra filtration. The purified C-dots in the dialysis bag were then transferred to another dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off value of 10000 Da to undergo another 24 h dialysis against ultra-pure water. The solution outside the dialysis bag was collected and underwent vacuum evaporation to produce the C-dots with the desired particle size distribution. The obtained C-dots were stored at 4 ℃ for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of C-dots doped PEBBLE (C-PEBBLE) nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The method for the synthesis of C-PEBBLE nanosensors ( Fig. 1) was developed based on the method for synthesizing conventional PEBBLE nanosensor in literatures [18,19]. The detailed procedures for the synthesis of C-PEBBLE nanosensors were as follows. 0.80 g AOT and 1.54 g Brij 30 were put in a clear and dry flask. 21.0 mL deoxygenated hexane was then quickly added with vigorous stirring under argon environment. 0.25 mL of the solution of the intensity-based fluorescence probe (i.e., 1.0 mg mL−1 Rhod-2 solution or 9.94 mg mL−1 acriflavine solution) and 0.7 mL monomer solution (prepared by dissolving 1.08 g acrylamide and 0.32 g N, N′-methylenebisacrylamide in 3 mL ultra-pure water) were added into the above mixture to form micro-emulsions system. Subsequently, 0.02 mL TEMED and 0.03 mL APS (10%) were added to initiate polymerization reaction under argon environment. After 1.5 h of polymerization, 0.01 mL APS (10%) and 0.01 mL of the as-prepared C-dots were added. The polymerization reaction continued for another one hour. Finally, the solvent n-hexane was removed by vacuum evaporation. The remaining content in the flask, i.e. the C-PEBBLE nanosensor, was re-suspended in ethanol and then filtered through a 50 nm organic membrane. The C-PEBBLE nanosensor collected was stored at −20 ℃. Two kinds of C-PEBBLE nanosensors were synthesized, the one encapsulating C-dots and acriflavine was a C-PEBBLE nanosensor for
NO
2
−
, the other one composed of C-dots and Rhod-2 was designed for the quantification of Ca2+.

The quantitation of
NO
2
−
in environmental water samples was used as a proof-of-concept example to demonstrate the performance of C-PEBBLEs in combination with the QFMGRP model. For the determination of
NO
2
−
in environmental water samples using the C-PEBBLE nanosensor encapsulating C-dots and acriflavine, twelve standard samples (7 calibration samples and 5 validation samples) with different concentrations of
NO
2
−
were prepared by diluting appropriate amounts of working solution of
NO
2
−
(35.94 μM) with ultrapure water ( Table 1). A real-world environmental water samples (R01) was collected from Xiangjiang River (Changsha, China). Another five spiked samples (SR01-SR05) were prepared by spiking the real-world environmental water samples with different concentrations of
NO
2
−
. Since the emission fluorescence spectrum of the intensity-based probe acriflavine is partially overlapped with that of the C-dots, the excitation fluorescence spectra of both acriflavine and C-dots can be measured at the same emission wavelength. For each sample, 750 μL of the sample was mixed with 150 μL H2SO4 (0.5 M) and 100 μL the working solution of C-PEBBLE nanosensor (0.89 mg mL−1) composed of C-dots and acriflavine (The amount of C-PEBBLE nanosensor was optimized based the concentration range of
NO
2
−
). The excitation spectrum of the mixture in the range of 320–480 nm at a resolution of 0.2 nm were measured by an F7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with the emission wavelength fixed at 500 nm, a scanning speed of 240 nm min−1, and slit widths of 5 nm on both excitation and emission sides. Each sample was analyzed three times.

Metal ions such as calcium (Ca2+) and magnesium (Mg2+) ions are essential mineral nutrients for life and present in every cell type in every organism. The determination of free Ca2+ and Mg2+ inside cells is of utmost importance. In this contribution, the C-PEBBLE nanosensor composed of C-dots and Rhod-2 was used to quantify the concentration of the free Ca2+ in a simulated system to illustrate the ability of the QFMGRP model in combination with C-PEBBLEs in dealing with the situation where both the excitation and emission spectra of C-dots are completely separated from the corresponding ones of the intensity-based probe (i.e. Rhod-2) encapsulated inside PEBBLE nanosensors. Fifteen standard samples including ten calibration samples (concentration levels of the free Ca2+: 0, 5.875, 19.73, 36.94, 59.00, 88.28, 129.0, 189.6, 258.7 and 289.2 nM) and five validation samples (concentration levels of the free Ca2+: 12.47, 27.84, 47.25, 72.54 and 106.8 nM) were prepared at 37 ℃ by mixing two solutions (solution A: 3 mM EGTA; solution B: 14.14 mM Ca2+ in 3 mM EGTA; both containing 100 mM KCl, 10 mM HEPES, pH 7.00) in various ratios. A real-world sample (R1) of Child-Life nature orange flavor liquid calcium/magnesium was prepared by diluting 0.2 mL of the primary liquid to 10 mL with HEPES buffer. Another five spiked samples (SR1–SR5) were prepared by spiking the real-world sample with different concentrations of Ca2+ (i.e., 0.2829, 0.5658, 0.8486, 1.132, and 1.273 mM). The final concentration of EGTA in each sample was kept at 3 mM, and the pH was adjusted to 7.00 with KOH. The corresponding free Ca2+ in the standard samples was calculated by the method proposed by Harrison and Bers [20]. For each sample, 700 μL of the sample was mixed with 20 μL working solution of the C-PEBBLE nanosensor (4.36 mg mL−1) composed of C-dots and Rhod-2 prepared with HEPES buffer and 200 μL 15 mM EGTA, and diluted to 1 mL by HEPES buffer solution. Please note, the amount of C-PEBBLE nanosensor added during the quantitative analysis of Ca2+ was optimized to get a good balance between the detection sensitivity and linear detection range for Ca2+ (data not shown). Since both the excitation and emission spectra of C-dots are completely separated from the corresponding ones of Rhod-2, it is impossible to acquire a fluorescence spectrum containing the spectral information of both the C-dots and Rhod-2 (Fig. S1, Supplementary materials). Therefore, a dual wavelength monitoring strategy was adopted. Two excitation spectra of the mixture in the range of 332–576 nm at emission wavelength 445 and 590 nm, respectively, were collected by an F7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with a resolution of 0.2 nm, a scanning speed of 240 nm min−1, and slit widths of 5 nm on both excitation and emission sides. The two excitation spectra were summed up to create a virtual spectrum for each sample, which was used for quantitative analysis of free Ca2+. Each sample was analyzed three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
The QFMGRP model for the quantitation of the total
NO
2
−
in samples using the C-PEBBLE nanosensor composed of the C-dots and acriflavine
ce:section.ce:para:
For the PEBBLE nanosensor composed of an intensity-based fluorescence probe (i.e., acriflavine) and an inert reference dye (i.e., the C-dots), its fluorescence spectrum (x
i ) in the presence of
NO
2
−
can be expressed as the linear combination of the fluorescence contributions of the inert reference dye and the remaining probe after reacted with
NO
2
−
.

2
−
denote the total concentrations of the reference dye, fluorescence probe, and
NO
2
−
in the i-th sample;
f
C − dots
and
f
probe
stand for the corresponding pure fluorescence spectra of C-dots and fluorescence probe (i.e., acriflavine), respectively.

For real-world samples, the presence of practically unavoidable scatterers and background absorbers can significantly distort the shape and intensity of fluorescence spectrum of the PEBBLE nanosensor. When taking both the scattering and background absorption effects into consideration, Eq. (1) has to be replaced by the following more complex expression [15].
(2)

is the concentration of the k-th absorber in the i-th sample and

k

denotes the absorbance spectrum of the k-th absorber modulated by wavelength-dependent effects of scatterers;

and

stands for wavelength-dependent-scattering- effect modulated pure fluorescence spectra of C-dots and fluorescence probe, respectively; b
i accounts for the multiplicative effect of scatters on the fluorescence spectrum of the i-th sample; d
i represents the multiplicative effects of scatterers on background absorption; ‘ ° ’ symbolizes the Hadamard product.

c

) are constant across samples. Under some mild conditions, a linear relationship exists between x

ce:section.ce:section-title: The QFMGRP model for the quantitation of the free Ca2+ in samples using the C-PEBBLE nanosensor encapsulating the C-dots and Rhod-2
ce:section.ce:para:
When using the PEBBLE nanosensor encapsulating a intensity-based fluorescence probe (i.e., Rhod-2) for Ca2+ (
Ca
2 +
+
probe
→
probe
−
Ca
2 +
) and an inert reference dye (i.e., the C-dots) to quantify the free Ca2+ in samples, two excitation spectra at emission wavelength 445 and 590 nm should be measured for each sample, and summed up to create a virtual spectrum. The virtual spectrum (x
i ) of the i-th sample can be expressed as the linear combination of the fluorescence contributions of C-dots, the free probe and the Ca2+ bound probe (probe-Ca2+).

i
β
2

For a test sample (x
test ), the value of
k
d
+
k
q
∙ [
Ca
2 +
]
test
k
d
+ [
Ca
2 +
]
test
can be estimated as
α
2
+
x
test
β
2
α
1
+
x
test
β
1
. The concentration of the free Ca2+ in the test sample (
[
Ca
2 +
]
test
) can then be readily calculated according the following equation.
(9)
[
Ca
2 +
]
test
=
k
d
∙ { α
1
−
α
2
+
x
test
(
β
1
− β
2
) }
α
2
−
k
q
∙ α
1
+ x
test
(
β
2
− k
q
β
1
)

The model parameters (i.e., the number of chemical components used in OPLECm and the numbers of underlying latent variables latent variables used in the two calibration models built by PLS) in the above QFMGRP calibration models for the quantification of
NO
2
−
and the free Ca2+ were optimized according to the criterion of minimal root-mean-square error of prediction (RMSEP) for the corresponding validation samples. The optimal QFMGRP calibration models were then used to determine the concentration of
NO
2
−
and the free Ca2+ in the corresponding real-world samples and spiked samples, respectively. For the purpose of comparison, the traditional univariate ratiometric model (URM) and conventional multivariable calibration method such as partial least square regression (PLS) were also investigated. The PLS calibration models were built on the same fluorescence spectral data and adopted the same criterion for model parameter optimization as used by QFMGRP. The quality characteristics of the QFMGRP, PLS and URM models for
NO
2
−
and the free Ca2+ in the calibration and validation samples were supplied in Tables S1 and S2 (Supplementary materials), respectively. The data analysis was performed using programs written in-house in Matlab version 7.0 (Mathworks, Inc).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the C-dots and C-PEBBLE nanosensors
ce:section.ce:para:
A shown in Fig. 2a, the particle size distribution of the as-prepared C-dots is about 2–4 nm. The C-dots emit strong blue fluorescence even at a very low concentration (the stock solution of C-dots was diluted in water to 1:5000) upon excitation by ultraviolet radiation at 365 nm (Fig. 2b). Its fluorescence intensity remains essentially unchanged even when large amounts of either
NO
2
−
or Ca2+ ( Fig. 3) were present. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots is also quite stable in the presence of various possible interferences such as Cu2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cr2+, NaAc, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, alcohol, glucose, melamine and L-sodium glutamate (Fig. S2, Supplementary materials). These facts verified the applicability of the C-dots as inert fluorescence references in the synthesis of PEBBLE nanosensors for the quantification of either
NO
2
−
or Ca2+.

During the synthesis of C-PEBBLE nanosensors, it was observed that if the C-dots and intensity-based probe were simultaneously added into the reactor, the resulting C-PEBBLE nanosensors would have strong signal of the C-dots and weak signal of the intensity-based probe. An intuitive explanation for this phenomenon is as follows. The PEBBLE nanosensors are of space grid structure formed by the free radical polymerization between acrylamide and N, N′-methylenebisacrylamide. The size of the grids in PEBBLE nanosensors would be stretched by the C-dots with a diameter of 2–4 nm, which leads to the leakage of intensity-based probe molecules of much smaller size out of the PEBBLE nanosensors.

In order to avoid such a situation, the intensity-based probe (Rhod-2 or acriflavine) was added into the reactor 1.5 h before the addition of the inert fluorescence reference C-dots. Because of the appropriate order of reagent addition, the obtained C-PEBBLE nanosensors had a core-shell like structure with small particles of C-dots embedded in the shell of PEBBLE nanosensors (about 200 nm in size) and the intensity-based probe encapsulated in the center. Fig. 4 demonstrated that many tiny dots embedded at the surface of a relative big nanoparticle, which was consistent with the synthesis theory of C-PEBBLE nanosensors: C-dots were encapsulated in the shell layer of C-PEBBLE nanosensors. The obtained C-PEBBLE nanosensors have decent fluorescence signals from both the inert C-dots and the intensity-based probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Quantitative determination of
NO
2
−
using the C-PEBBLE nanosensors composed of the C-dots and acriflavine
ce:section.ce:para:
NO
2
−
can react with acriflavine under an acidic condition to form a non-fluorescent compound [23–25]. The fluorescence intensity of acriflavine is inversely proportional to the concentration of
NO
2
−
( Fig. 5a). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of acriflavine is virtually constant in the presence of different kinds of interferences (Fig. 5b). Besides the interferences investigated in this contribution, according to reference 24, other interferences such as Ca2+ (250 mg L−1), Mg2+ (100 mg L−1),
SO
4
2
−
(900 mg L−1),
NO
3
−
(100 mg L−1) and
CO
3
2
−
(400 mg L−1) also have no significant effects on the fluorescence intensity of acriflavine. So acriflavine can be used as a intensity-based selective fluorescence probe for the determination of
NO
2
−
.

Fig. 6 displays the excitation fluorescence spectra of C-PEBBLE nanosensor (containing both the C-dots and acriflavine) in response to
NO
2
−
with different concentration levels. The height of the fluorescence peak of acriflavine centered at about 452 nm decreased along with the increase of
NO
2
−
concentration. The fluorescence peak at about 367 nm is the characteristic peak of the C-dots, which should remain unchanged under different concentration of
NO
2
−
. However, its height shows some degree of variation in response to the change in
NO
2
−
concentration due to the overlap between the excitation spectra of the C-dots and acriflavine, which will jeopardize the quantitative accuracy of the traditional univariate ratiometric model based on the fluorescence intensity ratio between the characteristic peaks of acriflavine and the C-dots.

Table 2 listed the quantitative results of the QFMGRP model, the PLS model, and the traditional univariate ratiometric model (URM) based on intensity ratios between two fluorescence peaks at 452 and 367 nm for
NO
2
−
in both the real-world environment water sample (R01) and spiked samples (SR01–SR05). The average concentrations of
NO
2
−
in sample R01 predicted by QFMGRP, PLS, and URM models were 0.567 ± 0.044, 0.323 ± 0.165 and 0.452 ± 0.041 μM, respectively. The concentration of
NO
2
−
in the real-world environment water sample was also quantified by an HPLC-UV method developed in literature [26,27]. The corresponding
NO
2
−
concentration determined by the HPLC-UV method (Supplementary material) was 0.609 ± 0.028 μM. Obviously, the prediction of QFMGRP model was very close to the value obtained by HPLC. The t-test at a confidence level of 95% confirmed that there was no significant difference between the average concentration predicted by QFMGRP for
NO
2
−
in the real-world sample and the corresponding reference value provided by HPLC. While the concentration predictions provided by both the PLS and URM models were significantly different from the reference value. Moreover, the average recovery rate of QFMGRP model for the spiked samples was 103 ± 4.8%, significantly better than the corresponding values of both the PLS model (148 ± 21%) and the URM model (113 ± 5%).

The reason for the success of the combination of the C-PEBBLE nanosensor with QFMGRP in providing rather accurate and precise concentration predictions for
NO
2
−
in the real-world sample R01 and spiked samples SR01–SR05 lies in the capability of QFMGRP in dealing with both the confounding effect on fluorescence measurements caused by the presence of scatterer (i.e., the C-PEBBLE nanosensor) and the problem caused by the overlapping between excitation spectrum of the C-dots and acriflavine. In contrast, the URM model could only partly mitigate the confounding effect on fluorescence measurement caused by the presence of scatterer due to the overlapping between the excitation spectra of the C-dots and acriflavine, and hence its concentration predictions were inferior to those of QFMGRP. As for the PLS model, it gave the worst concentration predictions because of its incompetence in modeling the confounding effect on fluorescence measurements caused by the presence of scatterer.

The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) of the C-PEBBLE nanosensor in combination with QFMGRP for
NO
2
−
in environmental water samples were estimated to be 0.012 and 0.035 μM, respectively, which were significantly lower than the corresponding values of HPLC method (i.e., 0.178 and 0.592 μM, respectively) as well as the upper limit of nitrite in drinking water (about 21.7 μM) suggested by China's drinking water standards (the third edition, 2011). So it can be concluded that the combination of QFMGRP with the C-PEBBLE nanosensor encapsulating the C-dots and acriflavine can realize sensitive and accurate determination of
NO
2
−
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of the free Ca2+ using the C-PEBBLE nanosensor encapsulating the C-dots and Rhod-2
ce:section.ce:para: It is impossible to collect either an excitation or emission spectrum containing the fluorescence signals of both the C-dots and Rhod-2 (Fig. S1, Supplementary materials). Though the fluorescence spectrum of the C-dots can be regulated by changing the synthesis conditions [28–30]. It is a rather difficult and time-consuming work to synthesize C-dots with a fluorescence spectrum having the desired degree of overlapping with that of the intensity-based fluorescence probe used in C-PEBBLE nanosensor. In this contribution, a dual-wavelength monitoring strategy was adopted to solve the problem caused by the complete separation between the fluorescence spectra of the C-dots and the intensity-based probe Rhod-2. Two excitation spectra of a sample at emission wavelength 445 nm and 590 nm were measured. After removing Rayleigh scattering, the two excitation spectra were summed up to create a virtual spectrum ( Fig. 7), which was then subject to quantitative analysis. As shown in Fig. 7, the variation in the concentration of the free Ca2+ had hardly any significant effect on the height of the characteristic peak of the C-dots centered at 367 nm, which indicated the appropriateness of using the C-dots as an inert reference in the C-PEBBLE nanosensor for the quantitation of the free Ca2+. The fluorescence intensity of Rhod-2 at 552 nm increased along with the increase of the free Ca2+ concentration. As a result, the C-PEBBLE nanosensor encapsulating both the C-dots and Rhod-2 can be used as a generalized ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor for the detection of the free Ca2+.

Fig. 8 showed the concentration predictions for the free Ca2+ in the calibration and validation samples obtained by the QFMGRP model in combination with the dual-wavelength monitoring strategy. Clearly, the QFMGRP model could provide quite satisfactory concentration predictions for the free Ca2+ in both the calibration and validation samples from their virtual fluorescence spectra measured by using the C-PEBBLE nanosensor encapsulating the C-dots and Rhod-2. The correlation coefficient between the concentration prediction of the QFMGRP model and the expected values was 0.997. It should be stressed that the QFMGRP model was dispensable for the success of the C-PEBBLE nanosensor encapsulating the C-dots and Rhod-2 in quantifying the concentration of the free Ca2+. Other calibration models such as PLS and traditional URM model based on the fluorescence intensity ratio between 552 and 367 nm could not effectively model the relationship between the concentrations of the free Ca2+ and the corresponding virtual fluorescence spectra, even for the standard samples (Fig. S3 and S4, Supplementary materials). Table 3 listed the quantitative results of the QFMGRP, PLS and URM models for the free Ca2+ in the real-world turbid sample and spiked samples (SR01–SR05). As can be seen in Table 3, the quantitative results of the QFMGRP model were creditable in good consistence with the corresponding expected values calculated from the quantitative results of an atomic absorption spectrophotometer (AAS) (Experimental details were provided in Supplementary materials). Its recovery rates for the spiked samples were in the range of 92–111%, which were superior to the corresponding values of either the PLS model or the URM model, especially for the spiked samples with relatively low concentrations of the free Ca2+. These results demonstrate that the combination of the QFMGRP model and dual-wavelength monitoring strategy can help realize accurate quantification of the free Ca2+ even when it is unfeasible to simultaneously acquire fluorescence signals of both the C-dots and the intensity-based probe Rhod-2.

C-dots have the advantages of excellent water-solubility, strong anti-photobleaching, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, high fluorescence quantum yield, as well as adjustable excitation and emission wavelength. In this contribution, C-dots were first used as generic inert references in the synthesis of generalized ratiometric PEBBLE nanosensors. PEBBLE nanosensors for the quantification of
NO
2
−
and the free Ca2+ in real-world samples were synthesized by encapsulating C-dots and intensity based fluorescence probes (i.e., acriflavine for
NO
2
−
, and Rhod-2 for the free Ca2+, respectively) inside the pores of stable matrix. With the help of a dual-wavelength monitoring strategy and a quantitative fluorescence model for generalized ratiometric probes (QFMGRP) based on PEBBLE technique, accurate quantification of
NO
2
−
and the free Ca2+ were achieved by the usage of the corresponding PEBBLE nanosensors, in spite of either strong overlapping between the emission (or excitation) spectra of the C-dots and the intensity-based probe or the unfeasibility of simultaneously acquiring fluorescence signals of both the C-dots and the intensity-based probe. The quantification results of the proposed strategy for
NO
2
−
and the free Ca2+ were in good consistence with those determined by HPLC and AAS, respectively. It should be stressed that QFMGRP was essential for the success of PEBBLE nanosensors with the C-dots as inert references. The combination of the QFMGRP model, C-dots and dual-wavelength monitoring strategy can greatly simply the synthesis of PEBBLE nanosensors, improve their quantitative results, and extend their applications as well.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21475039 and 21775038), the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (No. 2015JJ2028); the Scientific Research Foundation for Introduced Talent of Guizhou University (Grant Nos. 201564, and 201669), the Science and Technology Project of Guizhou Province (Grant No. [2017]7293), the Cultivation and Innovative Exploration Project for Young Scholar of Guizhou University (Grant No. [2017]5788), and the High Level Innovative Talents Training Program of Guizhou Province (No. 20165663) for financial supports.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.09.017.
